review_summary: Textual grounding is sufficient for concept-focused questions, and no separate video clip is needed.

question_bank.question: Why can a static word embedding fail on a word like “record”?
choices: It assigns one vector to the surface form, so different senses are blended together.
It cannot represent the word unless it appears at the start of a sentence.
It always treats nouns and verbs as separate vocabulary items.
It only stores the spelling of the word and no numerical values.
question_bank.explanation: A static embedding maps the same string to the same vector in every context. That makes it hard to distinguish meanings such as the noun “record” from the verb “record.”

question_bank.question: What distinguishes whole-model pretraining from the older approach of using pretrained word embeddings?
choices: Whole-model pretraining trains the contextual network parameters, not only the input embedding table.
Whole-model pretraining removes the need for downstream tasks.
Whole-model pretraining uses only labeled examples from the final task.
Whole-model pretraining freezes all weights before any language data is seen.
question_bank.explanation: Pretrained word embeddings initialize only the lookup vectors. Whole-model pretraining also trains the layers that compose contextual representations, giving the downstream model a much stronger starting point.

question_bank.question: Why can reconstructing masked text teach useful language representations?
choices: Predicting missing words forces the model to use syntax, semantics, co-reference, and world cues from context.
It teaches the model only to copy tokens from the input without learning context.
It prevents the model from learning anything about word order.
It works only when the missing token is the final word of a sentence.
question_bank.explanation: A missing-token objective creates many prediction problems from raw text. To solve them well, the model must use grammatical constraints, nearby meaning, entity references, and common facts encoded in context.

question_bank.question: Which example best matches the self-supervised pretraining recipe?
choices: Mask part of a naturally occurring sentence and train the model to predict the missing part.
Ask human annotators to label each sentence as positive or negative.
Train a classifier only on a small task-specific dataset.
Manually write grammar rules and use them as the model weights.
question_bank.explanation: Self-supervised pretraining obtains targets from the input itself. Masking text creates a prediction target without requiring a separate human annotation for each example.
